findings.claim: The Gumbel sampling kernel (_gumbel_sample_kernel in gpu/sample/gumbel.py) now upcasts logits to tl.float64 and computes the Gumbel noise in FP64 (via tl_rand64), and local_max reduction buffers are switched from float32 to float64. This is on a per-step hot path: gumbel_sample is the default sampling kernel invoked every decode step over the full [num_tokens, vocab_size] logits tensor. FP64 arithmetic on consumer/most datacenter GPUs (e.g. non-A100/H100 or with restricted FP64 units) runs at a small fraction (1/32 to 1/64) of FP32 throughput, and doubling the local_max buffer to float64 also increases the intermediate memory traffic on the vocab-parallel reduction. Q1(hot path): yes, every-step full-vocab sampling. Q3(default): yes — this is the default sampling path, not opt-in. However the change is for numerical fidelity of the double-log Gumbel transform (a legitimate correctness motivation), and the FP64 work is only the noise/log transform + local max, so the throughput impact depends on hardware FP64 capability. Confidence moderate because I cannot verify at parent whether gumbel_sample is unconditionally default vs gated, nor the exact GPU FP64 ratio in target deployment.

In _probabilistic_rejection_kernel (spec_decode/rejection_sampler.py) target_prob/draft_prob are now cast to tl.float64 and the uniform sample uses tl_rand64 (FP64). This runs per verified token in speculative decoding. FP64 comparison for the acceptance test is cheap relative to the full model forward, and this path only runs when spec decode is enabled (opt-in feature), so the runtime impact is small. Reporting only as a note, not an independent regression.

This commit flips the effective default of XPU graph (CUDA-graph-equivalent) from ENABLED to DISABLED for all XPU jobs. Previously, on the XPU platform, if supports_xpu_graph() was true and world_size_across_dp<=1, cudagraph_mode retained its configured value (typically graph capture enabled). Now the new `elif not envs.VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH:` branch unconditionally forces `compilation_config.cudagraph_mode = CUDAGraphMode.NONE` unless the user explicitly sets VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1. Three-question analysis: (1) Hot path? The affected path is the per-step decode/inference loop — disabling graph capture reintroduces per-step kernel-launch overhead, which is the canonical cudagraph-enablement/latency concern. (2) Caller? This code lives in XPUPlatform's compilation-config check (check_and_update_config style), invoked ONCE at engine init/config finalization, not per-step; but its EFFECT (cudagraph_mode=NONE) propagates to every subsequent decode step. (3) Default enabled? Yes — the new env var VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH defaults to False (int('0')), so the disabling branch is the default for every XPU user who does not opt in. This is a genuine throughput/latency regression on XPU decode workloads relative to the parent. HOWEVER, it is NOT reported as important because: (a) it is scoped exclusively to the Intel XPU platform (non-default hardware for the vast majority of vLLM users), (b) the commit message and warning indicate this is an intentional, likely-stability-motivated default change (XPU graph may be immature/buggy), which matches the 'correctness-forces-slow-path via intentional gating' pattern rather than an accidental regression, and (c) there is a clear documented off-switch (VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=1) preserving user opt-in. This is an intentional conservative default, not a silent misfire, so it is a suggestion for awareness only.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: minicpm.py MiniCPMMoE.forward flips fused_experts(..., inplace=True) -> inplace=False. This is on a hot path (per-MoE-layer, per-forward-step). With inplace=False, fused_experts allocates a fresh full-size output buffer (shape [num_tokens, hidden_size]) each forward instead of writing back into hidden_states in place, adding an extra large activation allocation per MoE layer per step. Q1(hot path): yes, MiniCPMMoE.forward runs every decoder layer that is MoE, every forward step. Q2(caller): MiniCPMMoE.forward is the standard MoE expert dispatch, called unconditionally in the model's decoder stack. Q3(default enabled): yes, this is the unconditional code path for MiniCPM MoE models with no gating flag. The parent used inplace=True; the diff makes the copy unconditional. This is a genuine per-step redundant-buffer allocation regression for MiniCPM MoE.

Commit flips `disable_cascade_attn` default from False (opt-out) to True (opt-in), disabling cascade attention for all V1 jobs that never set the flag. (1) Hot path: cascade attention runs in the attention forward, per-step per-layer — but only when the internal heuristic decides it is beneficial (long shared-prefix / high-batch reuse scenarios). (2) Caller: the flag lands in the V1 attention metadata builder which already gates actual usage behind a benefit heuristic, so this is not an unconditional per-step cost change — it only removes an already-conditional optimization. (3) Default config: yes, this now changes behavior for every default run. However, the PR's explicit intent is to trade a niche throughput optimization for numerical safety, and cascade attention was itself only conditionally engaged. Net effect is a potential throughput regression ONLY on shared-prefix workloads where the heuristic would have fired; typical decode/prefill is unaffected. Reported as suggestion since this is a deliberate, documented default-safety change rather than an accidental regression, and the affected path was already heuristic-gated (not always-on).
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: The new `_get_share_storage()` triggers `torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline(...)` — an nvcc/C++ JIT compile — on FIRST CALL of `discard_output_and_register_recompute`. Q1 (hot path?): the compile itself is lazy/one-time (guarded by `_share_storage_ext is None` cache), so it is NOT re-run per step; but it is invoked from `discard_output_and_register_recompute`, which per the activation-recompute mechanism runs per checkpointed forward. On the very first checkpointed forward this pays a full C++ extension build (invoking nvcc/g++, temp dir, ninja) inline in the training loop, adding tens of seconds of startup stall the first time recompute is exercised. Q3 (default enabled?): CheckpointWithoutOutput is used by TE-integrated activation-recompute paths; when recompute-without-output is active this code is on the default path, so the first-forward compile is unavoidable once the feature is on. The compile output also depends on the runtime toolchain being present at training time (fragile). This is a real (one-time-per-process) latency regression versus the previous pure-PyTorch storage copy which needed no compilation.

In `reduce_ipg_grads`/`ipg_remove_grads` path (stage_1_and_2.py ~line 998), the change adds a bidirectional cross-stream wait (`self.reduction_stream.wait_stream(current_stream())` and `current_stream().wait_stream(self.reduction_stream)`) before swapping the double ipg_buffer index. Analysis of the three questions: (1) Hot path? Partially — this runs when an IPG bucket fills up and is flushed, which happens multiple times per backward pass (per-bucket, not per-param), so it is on a per-step hot path but bounded by bucket count. (2) Caller? This block only executes when `self.contiguous_gradients and self.overlap_comm` is True AND `not get_accelerator().resolves_data_dependency()`. On CUDA accelerators `resolves_data_dependency()` typically returns True, so the added waits are skipped entirely on the common GPU path. (3) Default enabled? `overlap_comm` is opt-in and the guard `not resolves_data_dependency()` further restricts this to accelerators that don't auto-resolve stream dependencies. This is a correctness fix for a real data-race/sync bug (loss→0 at step 3) where the double-buffer index was swapped before the reduction stream finished reading the old buffer. The cross-stream wait_stream calls only add ordering enforcement (no full device sync) and are event-based, not a blocking D2H/host sync. Net: this is a correctness-motivated sync that is (a) guarded off on the dominant CUDA path and (b) enforces necessary ordering to prevent buffer reuse-before-read. Not a genuine perf regression; the small serialization it introduces is required for correctness and only on non-auto-dependency accelerators.

The commit adds build_for_cudagraph_capture() which calls seq_lens_cpu.max().item() — a device-to-host sync. However this runs only at CUDA graph *capture* time (a one-time setup path during warmup), not per replay step. The comment explicitly notes it uses actual max_seq_len instead of max_model_len to avoid recompilation during capture. Since capture is a cold/warmup path, this .item() is not on the steady-state replay hot path and is acceptable.

The new persistent buffers are sized by coarse upper bounds: persistent_physical_to_logical is [max_num_seqs, num_gpu_blocks] (int64), persistent_kv_indices is [max_model_len, max_num_kv_block] (int32). These are allocated once (lazy init on first build / in __init__), not per-step, so the allocation cost itself is one-time. But the [max_model_len, max_num_kv_block] shape can be large and is over-provisioned vs actual runtime extent; worth confirming it doesn't inflate steady-state memory footprint significantly. This is a memory-footprint sizing concern, not a per-step compute regression.

This commit is an OPTIMIZATION that removes a redundant copy, not a regression. Previously `moe_permute` unconditionally did `topk_ids.clone()` on every call. Now it aliases `topk_ids` directly and only clones inside the `if (expert_map.has_value())` branch (where `preprocessTopkIdLauncher` mutates the buffer in-place). Three-question analysis: (1) Hot path — yes, moe_permute runs per-forward on the MoE expert-routing path, and topk_ids scales with n_token*topk, so avoiding the clone saves a real per-step device alloc+copy. (2) Callers — moe_permute is the fused-MoE permutation entry invoked each MoE layer forward. (3) Default config — when expert_map is None (common non-EP case), the clone is now skipped entirely; correctness is preserved because sortAndScanExpert now takes `const int*` and does not mutate topk_ids. No regression introduced; the change is safe and beneficial.
